title: German Conjunctions
subtitle: A clear beachy guide to German conjunctions, word order, and meaning.
description: Explore coordinating, subordinating, and adverbial connectors with examples, tips, and usage notes.

sections.title: 1. The Regular Ones (Coordinating Conjunctions / Position 0)
sections.description: These conjunctions connect two main clauses without changing the normal word order. They sit outside the sentence in position 0.

rows.example: Ich möchte kommen, aber ich habe keine Zeit.
rows.note: If the subject and verb are the same in both clauses, you can drop them in the second clause.

rows.tip: Explains a reason and does not push the verb.
rows.example: Er schläft, denn er ist müde.
rows.note: Cannot be used at the absolute beginning of a sentence; it must connect to a previous clause.

rows.example: Ich koche und er wäscht ab.
rows.note: Like aber, you can drop the subject/verb in the second part if identical.

rows.tip: Used only after a negative statement to correct it.
rows.example: Das ist nicht rot, sondern es ist blau.
rows.note: Requires a preceding negative; otherwise use aber.

rows.example: Wir gehen ins Kino, oder wir bleiben zu Hause.
rows.note: Commas are optional before oder and und, unlike the others.

sections.title: 2. The Verb Pushers (Subordinating Conjunctions)
sections.description: These conjunctions create subordinate clauses and push the conjugated verb to the end.

rows.tip: The standard way to say because; verb goes to the end.
rows.example: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil es regnet.
rows.note: Spoken German sometimes treats weil like a regular conjunction, but that is incorrect in writing.

rows.tip: Used to report speech, thoughts, or feelings.
rows.example: Ich weiß, dass du klug bist.
rows.note: Don't confuse it with das. Dass is purely a connector.

rows.tip: Used for conditions or repeated events.
rows.example: Ich helfe dir, wenn ich Zeit habe.
rows.note: Cannot be used for a single event in the past; use als instead.

rows.tip: Used for a single past event.
rows.example: Als ich ein Kind war, spielte ich viel.
rows.note: If the verb pusher clause starts the sentence, the next clause must start with a verb.

rows.tip: Sets up a contrast or unexpected result.
rows.example: Er geht spazieren, obwohl er krank ist.
rows.note: Obgleich or obschon exist, but obwohl is the most common.

sections.title: 3. The Verb Magnets (Adverbial Conjunctions / Position 1)
sections.description: These words act like adverbs, taking position 1 and pulling the verb into position 2.

rows.tip: Gives the result of a previous statement.
rows.example: Es regnet. Deshalb bleibe ich hier.
rows.note: Can also be placed later in the sentence, losing the front-magnet effect.

rows.tip: Shows an action despite the first clause.
rows.example: Es regnet. Trotzdem gehe ich raus.
rows.note: Widely accepted in modern German even when used right after a comma without und.

rows.example: Die Wohnung ist schön. Außerdem ist sie billig.
rows.note: Functions like in addition in English.

rows.example: Wir essen. Danach gehen wir schlafen.
rows.note: Dann means next, danach means after that specific thing.

rows.tip: Gives a consequence if something doesn't happen.
rows.example: Beeil dich, sonst verpassen wir den Zug!
rows.note: Often paired with a command or strong suggestion.
